text: This article provides a critical evaluation of the diverse conceptualizations of illiberalism, proposing a refined definition that positions the concept as the direct negation of three foundational liberal democratic principles: limited power, state neutrality, and an open society. By shifting the focus from specific institutional failures to a broader ideational syndrome, the study clarifies the complex relationship between illiberalism, populism, and authoritarianism. The second part of the article examines the implications of this framework for empirical research and identifies nine distinct "roads to illiberalism"—authoritarian, traditionalist, religious, libertarian, nativist-nationalist, populist, paternalist, materialist-technocratic, and left-wing—demonstrating how varied ideological motives can converge in their opposition to the liberal democratic vision.
